title: Kubernetes Services & Networking Quiz
description: Test your knowledge on ClusterIP, LoadBalancers, Ingress, and internal DNS.

questions.question: Why can't applications rely on direct Pod IP addresses for communication?

options.text: Because they are encrypted using Base64.
Because Pod IP addresses are ephemeral (temporary). If a pod dies and is recreated, its IP changes.
Because Pod IPs are exposed to the public internet by default.
Because Kubernetes does not assign IP addresses to Pods.

questions.question: Which Kubernetes Service type is the default, and restricts access strictly to applications inside the cluster?

options.text: NodePort
LoadBalancer
ClusterIP
Ingress

questions.question: How does a Service object dynamically know which Pods it should route traffic to?

options.text: By hardcoding the Pod IDs in the Service YAML.
By matching the Service's strictly defined 'selector' against the Pods' 'labels'.
It automatically routes traffic to all pods in the same namespace.
By probing the pods using HTTP Health Checks.

questions.question: If you create a Service of type 'LoadBalancer' inside an AWS EKS cluster, what happens in the background?

options.text: Kubernetes makes an API call to AWS to provision a real, physical AWS Application Load Balancer and points it at your cluster.
Kubernetes installs Nginx inside a Master Node.
It opens a random high port (30000-32767) on all EC2 instances.
It creates a new VPC.

questions.question: What is the primary architectural advantage of using an 'Ingress' over multiple 'LoadBalancer' services?

options.text: It increases the security encryption level of the traffic.
It eliminates the need for NodePorts entirely.
It allows you to map multiple microservices to different URL paths behind a single public Load Balancer IP, significantly reducing cloud costs.
It provides offline caching mechanisms for static assets.
